coredata.dc:title: Polyaspartic acid modified by fluorescent carbon quantum dots as an environmentally friendly scale inhibitor for calcium sulphate
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tingting
Zhang, Daquan
Liu, Dongdong
Cai, Yijie
Gao, Lixin
coredata.dc:description: Polysuccinimide (PSI), as the precursor of polyaspartic acid (PASP), can easily react with amino (NH2) groups under mild conditions. A novel scale inhibitor, designed as PASP/N-CDs, is synthesised through the ring-opening reaction of PSI with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs). XPS, TEM, FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy are used to analyse the micro structure and spectral characteristics of synthesised inhibitors. The efficiency of PASP/N-CDs in inhibiting calcium sulphate (CaSO4) scale is tested through static scale inhibition experiments. The comparative analysis reveals a significant enhancement in the scaling resistance of PASP/N-CDs over PASP, particularly noticeable at reduced concentrations. The scale inhibition efficiency of PASP/N-CDs reaches nearly 100 % at 5 mg/L. The underlying mechanism of scale inhibition in PASP/N-CDs is investigated using SEM and XRD. It indicates that the PASP/N-CDs inhibits the formation of CaSO4 scale via a combination of chelation, adsorption, and lattice distortion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Seawater desalination
Modified polyaspartic acid
Carbon quantum dot
CaSO4

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Our work was supported by NSFC project (Grant No 52071198 ). We thank the grant from the Science & Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality ( 18DZ2204400 , 19DZ2271100 ) very much.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis route of PASP/N-CDs scale inhibitor.
Morphological characteristics of PASP/N-CDs. (a) TEM image of PASP/N-CDs. The inset (right) is a high-magnification image; (b) particle size distribution.
Structural characterizations of scale inhibitors. (a) Raman spectra of N-CDs; (b) FT-IR spectra of PASP and PASP/N-CDs.
XPS spectra of N-CDs. (a) The full scan survey spectrum. High-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (b), N 1s (c) and O 1s (d).
XPS spectra of PASP/N-CDs. (a) The full scan survey spectrum. High-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (b), N 1s (c) and O 1s (d).

Optical properties test results. The UV–vis spectra (black dash curve) and the PL spectra of N-CDs (a) and PASP/N-CDs (b). (c) The emission spectra of PASP/N-CDs with different concentrations at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. (d) Linear fitting result of emission intensity and concentration of PASP/N-CDs at 452 nm. The emission spectra of N-CDs (e) and PASP/N-CDs (f) after exposure to UV light for varying durations.
The impact of different conditions on the efficiency of PASP/N-CDs and PASP in inhibiting CaSO4. (a) raw material ratios; (b) scale inhibitor concentration; (c) heating time; (d) test temperature.
EIS measurements data. (a) Nyquist plots; (b) Bode plots.
XRD test patterns of CaSO4 scale.
SEM images of CaSO4 scale. (a, a′) Without scale inhibitor; (b, b′) with PASP; (c, c′) with PASP/N-CDs.
Possible inhibition mechanism of PASP/N-CDs for CaSO4 scale.
The EIS measurements data fitted by using the equivalent circuits.

head.ce:title: Polyaspartic acid modified by fluorescent carbon quantum dots as an environmentally friendly scale inhibitor for calcium sulphate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai 201306, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai 201306, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Engineering Research Centre of Energy-Saving in Heat Exchange Systems, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai 2001306, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Engineering Research Centre of Energy-Saving in Heat Exchange Systems

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Engineering Research Centre of Energy-Saving in Heat Exchange Systems, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai 2001306, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai 201306, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polysuccinimide (PSI), as the precursor of polyaspartic acid (PASP), can easily react with amino (NH2) groups under mild conditions. A novel scale inhibitor, designed as PASP/N-CDs, is synthesised through the ring-opening reaction of PSI with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs). XPS, TEM, FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy are used to analyse the micro structure and spectral characteristics of synthesised inhibitors. The efficiency of PASP/N-CDs in inhibiting calcium sulphate (CaSO4) scale is tested through static scale inhibition experiments. The comparative analysis reveals a significant enhancement in the scaling resistance of PASP/N-CDs over PASP, particularly noticeable at reduced concentrations. The scale inhibition efficiency of PASP/N-CDs reaches nearly 100 % at 5 mg/L. The underlying mechanism of scale inhibition in PASP/N-CDs is investigated using SEM and XRD. It indicates that the PASP/N-CDs inhibits the formation of CaSO4 scale via a combination of chelation, adsorption, and lattice distortion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, seawater desalination technology driven by renewable energy has received more attention. The requirements for new-type seawater treatment scale inhibitors are also being updated and iterated. Scaling is one of the most serious problems in industrial production. Scale deposition occurs in various industrial settings, such as heat exchangers, water treatment plant facilities, reverse osmosis membrane surfaces, and seawater desalination pipelines [1–4]. The accumulation of scale within industrial equipment not only obstructs fluid flow but also hampers heat exchange efficiency [5]. Furthermore, the presence of scale can initiate corrosion processes, potentially leading to severe accidents [6]. In industrial production, the main scaling constituents commonly found are calcium carbonate (CaCO3) and calcium sulphate (CaSO4). The CaCO3 scale can be readily removed through acid treatment [7]. However, the CaSO4 scale poses a challenge for effective removal [8]. The addition of scale inhibitors is an effective preventative measure against scale formation [9].

Traditional phosphorus-containing scale inhibitors have gained widespread usage in various industries [10]. Nonetheless, their overuse poses risks related to the eutrophication of water ecosystems and subsequent environmental contamination [11]. Consequently, there is a growing focus on the development of environmentally friendly scale inhibitors [12]. Polyaspartic acid (PASP) is a representative of “green” scale inhibitors due to its non-toxicity and excellent biodegradability [13]. However, its suboptimal effectiveness in preventing CaSO4 scale restricts its industrial use. The readily cyclizable, highly active linear polysuccinimide (PSI) can be transformed into PASP with side chains by amino acids [14]. This provides a viable method for synthesising functionalized PASP. Numerous studies have incorporated different functional groups into the side chains of PASP, such as carboxyl (COOH) [15], hydroxyl (OH) [16], and sulfonyl (SO3H) [17]. Its improvement in CaSO4 scaling inhibition effectiveness is yet insufficient to fulfil the demand for real-world applications. Previous study has reported a 2 % difference in CaSO4 scale inhibition efficiency for CaSO4 between AEM/PASP (4-(2-aminoethyl) morpholine graft polyaspartic acid) and PASP [18]. There is an urgent need to explore novel approaches to developing high-efficiency scale inhibitors based on PASP [19].

In recent years, research on nanomaterials as green-scale inhibitors has gradually emerged [20]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received significant attention due to their negligible toxicity, diverse sources of raw materials, and remarkable optical properties [21]. Their fluorescence performance makes them ideal for online concentration monitoring [22]. Wang et al. [23] developed carboxyl-modified carbon quantum dots (CCQDs) by utilising citric acid as a raw material. The CCQDs possess a surface rich in hydroxyl (OH) and carboxyl (COOH) groups. However, a higher dosage of CCQDs is required to demonstrate good scale inhibition effects. Moreover, it was noted that CCQDs can lead to distortions in the crystal lattice of CaSO4. Further investigation is required to explore this phenomenon.

Herein, we prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) via a previously reported route [24]. Compared to undoped CDs, N-CDs exhibits an enhanced fluorescence quantum yield and its surface is rich in amino groups [25,26]. The N-CDs are incorporated into the ring-opening reaction of PSI, and the PASP/N-CDs scale inhibitor is successfully synthesised. This method is straightforward and under mild reaction conditions. It has been widely used for the synthesis of PASP modifications [18,27,28]. The synthesised samples are characterised by means of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), the Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and Raman spectroscopy. The commendable scaling inhibition effectiveness is certified through the static scale inhibition approach. Following this, the scaling inhibition mechanism is investigated through X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) and Scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

No additional purification was performed on the chemicals, as they were utilised in their original state. Urea, potassium hydroxide (KOH), and PSI (M W = 8000–10,000) were provided by McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Anhydrous ethanol, citric acid (CA), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were accessed by Sinopharm Reagent Co., Ltd. Calcium chloride (CaCl2) was bought from XiLong Scientific Co., Ltd. Sodium sulphate (Na2SO4) was from Shanghai Zhanyun Chemical Co., Ltd.

The N-CDs were prepared using a technique previously described [24]. In brief, 2 g of CA and 1.9 g of urea were mixed in 50 mL of deionized water. After fully dissolved, the entire mixture was placed into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at a temperature of 160 °C for 4 h. Subsequently, the mixture was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to eliminate larger particles, followed by ethanol precipitation and centrifugation. Additional purification was achieved using dialysis. The end product, a black solid, was acquired by drying the sample at 60 °C and storing it in sealed vials at 2 °C.

Initially, 0.6 g of PSI were blended with 10 mL of deionized water while stirring, creating a suspension. Subsequently, the different amounts of N-CDs (0.3, 0.06, 0.045 g) were added to the solution, followed by adding 5 mL of a 1 M NaOH immediately. The mixture was stirred at 60 °C for 24 h, after which the pH was adjusted to neutral using hydrochloric acid. The solution was then precipitated using anhydrous ethanol for 12 h and dried at 60 °C to give the PASP/N-CDs solid. PASP was synthesised using a similar method, except without the addition of N-CDs [29].

The morphology of PASP/N-CDs in aqueous solution was observed using a JEM-2100F Transmission electron microscope (TEM). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra and UV–visible (UV–vis) spectra were tested using an FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer and a U-2600 spectrophotometer. A Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was used to check the surface elemental composition of PASP/N-CDs and N-CDs. The functional groups of the scale inhibitors were characterised using an IS50 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR). The micro-morphologies of the CaSO4 scale were characterised with a Sigma 300 scanning electron microscope (SEM). Phase analysis of the CaSO4 crystals was performed by a Bruker-D8 X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD).

The method for static scale inhibition tests on CaSO4 referred to the National Standard of China (GB/T 16632-2019) [19]. All test solutions contained 2000 mg/L of calcium ions and 4800 mg/L of sulphate ions, with a pH value maintained at 8.8 ± 0.1. The effects of the CaSO4 scale inhibitor concentration, heating time, and temperature were measured, separately. The heated solutions were filtered to remove scale, and the supernatant was titrated with 0.01 mol/L ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA). The concentration of calcium ions in the supernatant was calculated based on the volume of EDTA consumed during the titration. The scale inhibition efficiency was calculated using the following formula:
(1)
η =
ρ 2
−
ρ 1
ρ 0
−
ρ 1
× 100 %
where ρ2 and ρ1 (mg/mL) represent the remaining calcium ions concentrations with and without the presence of inhibitors, respectively. The initial calcium ions concentration is denoted as ρ0 (mg/mL).

The AA5052 aluminium alloy was selected as the working electrode, with an exposed area of 1 cm2. Prior to the electrochemical tests, the electrode was sequentially polished using SiC papers ranging from 400 to 2000 grit, then rinsed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol to remove debris, and air-dried at ambient temperature. All electrochemical experiments were conducted on a CS310M electrochemical workstation. The EIS measurements were carried out within a certain frequency range, from 105 Hz to 10−1 Hz. The EIS test involved a perturbation amplitude of approximately 10 mV [30].

Fig. 1 shows the synthesis process for the PASP/N-CDs scale inhibitor. There is a lot of amino clusters on the N-CDs surface. The PASP attaches to the N-CDs surface via CONH covalent bonding. Consequently, the synthesised PASP/N-CDs product is abundant in amide and carboxyl groups, contributing to its ability to impede scale formation.

Fig. 2 illustrates the TEM images of PASP/N-CDs in an aqueous solution. The PASP/N-CDs present as dispersions and have a size of about 9.2 nm–13.5 nm (Fig. 2b). The sharp clarity TEM image (inset in Fig. 2a) displays distinct lattice fringes with a distance of 0.24 nm. It matches the (1120) crystal phase of graphite [31].

The Raman, FT-IR, and XPS spectra are performed to further confirm the structural information of the scale inhibitors. The Raman spectral data of N-CDs reveal two different peaks at 1343 cm−1 (D-band) and 1564 cm−1 (G-band) (Fig. 3a). The G-band is indicative of graphite's single crystallite structure, while the D-band typically signifies amorphous carbon or distortions in hexagonal rings [32]. Additionally, the D to G band intensity ratio serves as an indicator of the graphitization level in carbon-based materials. The Id/Ig ratio for the N-CDs stands at 0.99, suggesting a highly crystalline composition in the synthesised N-CDs [33].

The FT-IR spectra of PASP and PASP/N-CDs are shown in Fig. 3b. In PASP, the stretching vibrations for NH, CO, and CN in the amide group are located at 3441 cm−1, 1597 cm−1, and 1396 cm−1, respectively. This confirms the successful synthesis of PASP [34]. In the FT-IR spectrum of PASP/N-CDs, the stretching vibration bands of the amide group for NH, CO, and CN are at 3433 cm−1, 1604 cm−1, and 1404 cm−1. Furthermore, supplementary Fig. S1 presents the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs. The characteristic absorption bands of NH is at 3000–3500 cm−1. The shoulder peak at 1712 cm−1 is assigned to the CO bond in the carboxyl groups of N-CDs [24], and an analogous peak is evident in the spectrum of PASP/N-CDs. This indicates that N-CDs are involved in the ring-opening reaction of PSI to give PASP/N-CDs.

Figs. 4 and 5
show the XPS results of N-CDs and PASP/N-CDs. The prominent peaks in N-CDs are recognised for C 1s peaks at 284 eV, N 1s peaks at 400 eV, and O 1s peaks at 532 eV (Fig. 4a), respectively. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of N-CDs is shown in Fig. 4b. The spectrum shows three Gaussian peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.7 eV, and 288.7 eV. The sp2 carbon (CC/CC) in graphene, the sp3 carbon (CO/CN), and the CO in the carboxyl group are represented by these peaks [35]. The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum of N-CDs can be equipped with three Gaussian peaks at 399.8 eV, 400.4 eV and 401.6 eV (Fig. 4c) that align with CNC, N(C)3 and NH, respectively [36]. Moreover, the binding energies of 531.9 eV and 533.6 eV are the two main signals for O 1s, corresponding to CO and COH/COC [37]. Based on FT-IR and XPS spectra, it can be inferred that N-CDs contains functional groups such as carboxyl, amino, and aromatic CN hetero cycles. As shown in Fig. 5, the reaction between PSI and N-CDs affects the XPS spectra of PASP/N-CDs. In the spectra, the carbon elements are detected at different energy levels: CC/CC at 284.8 eV, CN/CO at 286.0 eV, and CO at 288.0 eV. The nitrogen elements are identified at distinct peaks: CNC at 399.2 eV, CNH at 399.7 eV, and NH/N(C)3 at 400.5 eV. The oxygen elements are detected as CO (531.2 eV) and COH/COC (532.3 eV). Additionally, the presence of sodium is due to the addition of NaOH during the synthesis process.

Fig. 6 shows the optical properties test results of N-CDs and PASP/N-CDs. The PL spectra and UV–vis spectra of N-CDs and PASP/N-CDs are shown in Fig. 6a and b. There are two distinct absorption bands in the UV–vis spectra of PASP/N-CDs and N-CDs, located at 234 nm and 333 nm. These absorption bands correspond to the CC and CO bonds' π → π* and n → π* transitions [38]. The maximum emission peak of N-CDs is observed at 360 nm PL excitation, while it is at 380 nm for PASP/N-CDs. The N-CDs reflect a consistent, narrow PL emission peak at 440 nm that is independent of excitation. This indicates uniformity in both their size and surface state [39]. The Em of PASP/N-CDs changes from 438 nm to 452 nm in the PL spectrum as the Ex moves from 340 nm to 380 nm. This may be ascribed to the surface modification of N-CDs by PASP [40]. When PASP/N-CDs is dissolved in water, it emits a visually detectable blue fluorescence when exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp. (inset in Fig. 6b).

The relationship between the emission intensity of the PASP/N-CDs and its concentration is studied under an excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. 6c). Subsequently, the emission intensity at 452 nm is selected for linear fitting with concentration (Fig. 6d). The results indicate that within the 1 mg/L to 10 mg/L concentration range, the emission peak intensity of PASP/N-CDs shows a linear correlation with its concentration (R2 = 0.99501). This linear relationship can be utilised for real-time monitoring of PASP/N-CDs concentrations in industrial water systems.

Photostability is a crucial property for assessing the potential applications of fluorescent materials [41]. Fig. 6e and f display the emission spectra of N-CDs and PASP/N-CDs under different UV excitation. After 90 min of exposure to 365 nm UV light, there is no significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of either N-CDs or PASP/N-CDs. This confirms their excellent photostability [42].

Static scale inhibition experiments are conducted to compare the performance of PASP/N-CDs and PASP in preventing CaSO4 scale formation. Firstly, the scaling inhibition effectiveness of the PASP/N-CDs obtained with different ratios of PSI:N-CDs is investigated (Fig. 7a). All test solutions are heated at 70 °C for 6 h. The PASP/N-CDs synthesised from 0.6 g PSI and 0.3 g N-CDs exhibit minimal improvement in inhibition performance when compared to PASP. However, when the ratio is adjusted to 0.6 g PSI: 0.06 g N-CDs, the PASP/N-CDs demonstrates a substantial enhancement in inhibition efficiency, achieving an inhibition efficiency of nearly 94 % at a concentration of 4 mg/L. Further increases in the proportion of PSI within the raw materials result in a decreased efficiency of scale inhibition in the resulting PASP/N-CDs. Therefore, the PASP/N-CDs used in subsequent tests are all synthesised from 0.6 g PSI and 0.06 g N-CDs.

Fig. 7b shows the scaling inhibition effectiveness of PASP/N-CDs and PASP at different concentrations. The heating temperature (70 °C) and duration (6 h) are kept constant throughout the experiment. The inhibition performance of PASP/N-CDs is consistently better than that of PASP. At a concentration of 3 mg/L, PASP/N-CDs achieves a scale inhibition effectiveness of 79 %. The PASP/N-CDs with a concentration of 5 mg/L reaches an inhibition efficiency as high as 100 %. This suggests that the growth of CaSO4 scale in the solution is significantly inhibited by PASP/N-CDs. Because of the abundant carboxyl groups in PASP/N-CDs, they can form stable chelates with calcium ions. The reduced probability of collisions between calcium ions and sulphate ions in the solution leads to the inhibited growth of CaSO4 crystals [43].

In Fig. 7c, PASP/N-CDs with a concentration of 6 mg/L maintains nearly 100 % scale inhibition efficiency against CaSO4 scale even after heating for 30 h at 70 °C. However, the efficacy of scale inhibition of PASP at the same concentration significantly decreases with prolonged heating time. This suggests that the chelates formed between PASP/N-CDs and calcium ions are more stable compared to those of PASP, enabling continuous inhibition of CaSO4 scale deposition.

The impact of temperature on scale inhibition's effectiveness is evaluated at a 6 mg/L dosage of the scale inhibitor and a heating period of 6 h (Fig. 7d). The PASP/N-CDs maintains an inhibition efficiency of over 90 % in the temperature range of 70 °C–85 °C, effectively inhibiting the growth of CaSO4 scale. The PASP's scale inhibition effectiveness significantly decreases with increasing temperature, particularly when the temperature increases to 85 °C from 80 °C. Furthermore, the performance of PASP/N-CDs on the inhibiting scale is consistently superior to that of PASP throughout the entire testing process. This suggests that PASP/N-CDs is more effective in combining with calcium ions and inhibiting the formation of crystals [44].

Furthermore, we tested the effectiveness of PASP/N-CDs in inhibiting CaCO3 scaling using methods reported in previous study [27]. The results are shown in supplementary Fig. S2. It is evident that the inhibition efficiency of PASP/N-CDs is about 10 % higher than that of PASP, indicating its potential as an industrial scale inhibitor.

To further explore the effects of various scale inhibitors on electrochemically deposited CaSO4 scale, the EIS measurements are performed and displayed in Fig. 8 . As shown in Fig. 8a, the Nyquist plots reveal a semicircular shape, suggesting that a charge transfer mechanism is in charge of the aluminium electrode's electrochemical response. At high frequencies, the capacitive loop is usually equivalent to the deposit of calcium scale and products of corrosion on the electrode's surface [45]. In the absence of scale inhibitors, the diameter of the loop is at its maximum, indicating the deposition of CaSO4 on the electrode surface, thereby impeding the transfer of charge. Upon the addition of scale inhibitors, the impedance of the electrode substantially decreases, suggesting decreased deposition of CaSO4 scale on the metal surface.

Fig. 8b represents the Bode plots for aluminium alloy electrodes in solutions both with and without scale inhibitors. The plots reveal that in the presence of scale inhibitor, the low frequency impedance modulus (|Z|0.1Hz) decreases. Additionally, only one time constant appears across the entire frequency range. Without an inhibitor, this peak of phase angle extends over a wide frequency range, suggesting that it is composed of the resistance from both the scale layer and the alumina layer. In the presence of scale inhibitors, the phase angle peak changes narrowly. These results imply that the presence of inhibitors reduces the coverage of CaSO4 on the electrode surfaces.

Analysing the impedance spectra is done by using appropriate equivalent circuits (inset in Fig. 8a) that comprises the solution resistance (Rs), the charge transfer resistance (Rct), and the constant phase element (CPE). Table 1 shows the impedance parameters fitted by using the equivalent circuits. It can be observed that the Rct during the PASP/N-CD solution is slightly higher than that of the PASP solution. This is explained by PASP/N-CDs forming a coating on the electrode surface. The presence of the PASP/N-CDs film impedes the charge transfer of aluminium alloy.

Fig. 11 shows the mechanism by which PASP/N-CDs inhibits the growth of CaSO4. The carboxyl groups present in PASP/N-CDs coordinate with calcium ions to form soluble chelates (Fig. 11a). Meanwhile, PASP/N-CDs adheres to the surfaces of CaSO4 crystals, enhancing the repulsion between particles. As a result, the CaSO4 microcrystals are evenly dispersed in the solution. Furthermore, the accelerated motion of carbon quantum dots intensifies the disintegration of CaSO4 crystals. This results in irregular growth and lattice distortion in CaSO4 (Fig. 11b). Consequently, PASP/N-CDs is highly effective in preventing the formation of CaSO4 scale.

In this work, we synthesize the PASP/N-CDs scale inhibitor capable of effectively inhibiting CaSO4 scale formation through the reaction between N-CDs and PSI. The fluorescence intensity of PASP/N-CDs in an aqueous solution is linearly related to its concentration. It can be used for online concentration monitoring. It demonstrates that the scaling inhibition effectiveness of PASP/N-CDs is better than that of PASP. As demonstrated through SEM and XRD studies, PASP/N-CDs can adhere to the CaSO4 scale crystal surface to hinder the formation of the scale. The rapid movement of N-CDs leads to the lattice deformation of the CaSO4 crystal. It has great industrial application prospects as scale inhibitor.

Tingting Zhang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Daquan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Dongdong Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yijie Cai: Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Lixin Gao: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Our work was supported by NSFC project (Grant No 52071198). We thank the grant from the Science & Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (18DZ2204400, 19DZ2271100) very much.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2024.117740.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Calcium sulfate dihydrate (gypsum) scale formation on heat exchanger surfaces: the influence of scale inhibitors
J. Colloid Sci.

Desalination

J. Membr. Sci.

Desalination
